unit.title: Essen & Trinken
unit.sub: Order food and drinks like a local — from the Bäckerei to the Biergarten.

steps.title: What'll You Have? ☕
steps.desc: Germans take their coffee and beer seriously! Learn to order drinks and basic food items.

steps.note: Masculine (der). Germans drink A LOT of coffee — 4th highest consumption in the world! 'Kaffee und Kuchen' (coffee and cake) at 3 PM is a tradition.
Masculine (der). East Friesland (Ostfriesland) has the highest tea consumption in the world — even more than the British!
Neuter (das). Germany has the Reinheitsgebot (Beer Purity Law) from 1516 — beer may only contain water, barley, and hops!
Masculine (der). German Riesling is world-famous! 'Rotwein' = red wine, 'Weißwein' = white wine — compound words, very German.
Neuter (das). In Germany, tap water is rarely served free at restaurants — you usually order 'Mineralwasser' (sparkling) or 'stilles Wasser' (still).
Feminine (die). 'Vollmilch' = whole milk, 'Magermilch' = skim milk. German dairy is excellent!

steps.title: ☕ Ordering drinks in Germany
steps.text:
Essential ordering phrases:

• Ich möchte einen Kaffee, bitte. — I'd like a coffee, please.
• Ein Bier, bitte! — A beer, please!
• Kann ich ein Glas Wasser haben? — Can I have a glass of water?
• Noch eins, bitte. — Another one, please.

⚠️ Important: In restaurants, water is NOT free!
'Leitungswasser' = tap water (free but unusual to order)
'Mineralwasser' = mineral water (what you'll get)
'Mit Kohlensäure' = sparkling
'Ohne Kohlensäure' / 'still' = still

steps.q: How do you order a coffee in German?

steps.title: Guten Appetit! 🍞
steps.desc: German food is hearty! Learn the essential food words — from Brot to Wurst.

steps.note: Neuter (das). Germany has over 3,200 registered bread varieties — more than any country on Earth! 'Brötchen' (bread roll) is the diminutive.
Masculine (der). 'Käsebrot' = cheese sandwich (literally 'cheese-bread'). Very common for 'Abendbrot' (evening bread = dinner).
Neuter (das). Germany is famous for 'die Wurst' (sausage) — Bratwurst, Currywurst, Weißwurst. Over 1,500 types of Wurst exist!

steps.exampleEn: I eat a lot of vegetables.
steps.note: Neuter (das). Always singular in German! 'Viel Gemüse' (many vegetables), not 'viele Gemüse'. Kartoffeln (potatoes) are Germany's most beloved vegetable.
Neuter (das). Literally 'early piece' (früh + Stück). German breakfast = bread, cheese, cold cuts, boiled egg, jam, and strong coffee.
Neuter (das). Many Germans eat 'Abendbrot' (cold bread with toppings) rather than a hot meal. The warm meal is often lunch ('Mittagessen').

steps.title: 🍽️ German food culture
steps.text:
Germans eat differently than you might expect:

🌅 Frühstück (breakfast): Hearty! Bread, cheese, cold cuts, eggs, muesli
☀️ Mittagessen (lunch): Usually the MAIN warm meal
🌙 Abendessen/Abendbrot (dinner): Often cold — bread with toppings

'Guten Appetit!' = Enjoy your meal! (said before eating)
'Prost!' = Cheers! (for beer)
'Zum Wohl!' = To your health! (for wine)

Fun fact: 'Abendbrot' literally means 'evening bread'. It's so common that it became the word for dinner!

deepDive.title: Germany's bread obsession
deepDive.text:
Germany is officially the bread capital of the world with over 3,200 registered bread varieties.

In 2014, German bread culture was added to the UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage list!

Popular types:
• Schwarzbrot — dark rye bread
• Vollkornbrot — whole grain bread
• Pumpernickel — dense, dark, slightly sweet
• Brötchen — bread rolls (called 'Semmel' in Bavaria!)

Germans abroad often say the thing they miss most is the bread. There are even German bakeries in places like NYC and Tokyo catering to homesick expats.

steps.title: At the Restaurant 🍽️
steps.desc: Order a full meal in German! From getting a table to paying the bill.

steps.note: Feminine (die). Simply say 'Die Rechnung, bitte' or 'Zahlen, bitte' (Pay, please). Tipping: 5-10% is normal, round up to the nearest euro.
'Möchte' (would like) + infinitive is the polite way to order. 'Ich möchte bestellen' = I'd like to order.
Feminine (die). Literally 'food-card'. Also: 'die Karte' (short form). In Bavaria you might hear 'die Menuekarte'.
One of the most useful German words! 'Lecker!' on its own = 'Yummy!' Very close to Dutch 'lekker'.

steps.title: 🍽️ At a German restaurant
steps.text:
Useful restaurant phrases:

• Einen Tisch für zwei, bitte. — A table for two, please.
• Die Speisekarte, bitte. — The menu, please.
• Ich möchte gerne bestellen. — I'd like to order.
• Was empfehlen Sie? — What do you recommend?
• Die Rechnung, bitte. — The bill, please.
• Stimmt so! — Keep the change!

💡 Tipping in Germany: 5–10% is standard.
Round up: if the bill is €18.40, you might say 'Zwanzig' (twenty) and leave €20.
You tell the server directly — don't leave money on the table!

steps.q: How do you ask for the bill?
